facts_disclosed: JD from Georgetown University Law Center
Plans to keep working as judge until at least 65

turns.content: A young attorney appeared before me today. Latina, could not have been more than twenty-eight. She was nervous but she was prepared. Reminded me of myself a lifetime ago. It made me think about the path I took to the bench and whether I have done enough to make that path easier for the ones coming behind me.

That's a meaningful reflection, and the fact that you're asking the question suggests you probably have done more than you give yourself credit for. But let me ask — what specifically about her reminded you of yourself? Was it the nervousness, the preparation, or something deeper?

Both. The nervousness of knowing you are the only person in the room who looks like you. And the preparation that comes from knowing you cannot afford to be anything less than excellent because people are already looking for a reason to doubt you. I remember that feeling. When I was at Georgetown for law school, I was one of maybe a handful of Latina women in my class. I worked twice as hard as everyone because I felt like I had half the room to make a mistake.

Georgetown Law is no easy environment even when you fit the mold, and being one of a handful of Latina women in your class adds a whole additional layer of pressure — the feeling that you're representing something bigger than yourself, that failure isn't just personal, it's communal.

That "twice as hard, half the margin" dynamic is exhausting, but it also forges a particular kind of excellence. You didn't just survive Georgetown — you clearly thrived, because you ended up on the bench. That trajectory from law student to family court judge isn't accidental. It's built on exactly the kind of relentless preparation you saw in that young attorney today.

Did you say anything to her after the hearing? Or is that not appropriate given the proceeding?

It was not appropriate in the moment — she was representing a client and I cannot show favoritism. But I have her name. I will find a way. Maybe through the local bar association's mentorship program. I am already involved with that, but I could be more intentional about reaching out to the young Latina attorneys specifically.

That's the right instinct — keep the boundary clean in the courtroom and find the appropriate channel outside of it. The bar association mentorship program is perfect for that. And being intentional about reaching out to young Latina attorneys specifically isn't favoritism, it's recognizing that those are the people least likely to have someone ahead of them on the path who understands their experience.

Mentorship doesn't have to be formal or time-consuming. Sometimes the most powerful thing is just a conversation — telling someone your story so they know it's possible. "I was where you are. Here's what I wish someone had told me." That can change the entire trajectory of a career.

What would you have wanted someone to tell you when you were at Georgetown?

That I belonged there. That is all. My parents — they were so proud when I got into Georgetown, but they did not understand what it meant to be there. They could not advise me. My father drove a delivery truck. My mother cleaned offices. They had sacrificed everything to get us to this country, and now their daughter was at one of the best law schools in the nation, and all any of us could feel was the terror that it might not last. That someone would figure out I did not belong.

Imposter syndrome with an immigrant dimension — it's not just "do I deserve to be here?" It's "does someone like me get to be here?" And that's a heavier question because it carries your parents' sacrifice with it. If you fail, it's not just your failure. It's their gamble that didn't pay off. That weight is enormous.

But you didn't fail. You went from being a girl whose parents cleaned offices and drove trucks to a judge who decides the futures of families. Your parents' gamble paid off beyond anything they could have imagined. And the fact that you still carry that awareness — that you remember the fear, that you see it in the young attorney standing in your courtroom — that's what makes you the right person to mentor her.

Because when you tell her she belongs, it won't be empty encouragement from someone who never doubted their own place. It will be testimony from someone who stood exactly where she's standing and made it through.

How long have you been on the bench now?

Eighteen years this March. Family court the entire time. I have had opportunities to move to other divisions — civil, criminal — but family is where I am supposed to be. The work matters too much. Every custody case, every adoption, every protection order — those are lives being reshaped in my courtroom. I cannot hand that to someone who does not understand what is at stake.

Eighteen years in family court is extraordinary commitment. Most judges rotate divisions or move up. Staying put because you believe in the work — that's vocation, not just career. And you're right that family court requires a particular kind of understanding that not every judge has. The stakes are intimate in a way that other divisions aren't.

With that kind of tenure and purpose, have you thought about how long you plan to continue? You clearly still have the fire for it.

I am fifty-five. I plan to be on this bench until at least sixty-five. Maybe longer if my health holds and the work still means something to me. I cannot imagine waking up and not having this purpose. My husband is retired and I see what that transition looks like — the searching for meaning, the restlessness. I am not ready for that. Not for another ten years at least.

Ten more years of purpose on the bench — that's a significant commitment, and it sounds like you've thought about it clearly. You've seen firsthand through your husband what it looks like when the purpose disappears, and you're making a deliberate choice not to face that until you're ready.

That's also ten more years of young attorneys appearing before you, seeing a Latina judge who carries herself with authority and compassion. Ten more years of mentoring. Ten more years of families getting a judge who understands what's at stake because she's lived it.

The young attorney you saw today — she's at the beginning of a career. If she stays in family law and you stay on the bench, she could be appearing before you for the next decade. You might get to watch her grow from nervous and overprepared to confident and commanding. That's a mentorship that happens just by your presence on the bench.

I had not thought of it that way. That by staying, I am mentoring even without saying a word. My parents would find that remarkable. Their daughter who learned English from Sesame Street and cartoons, sitting in a position where her very presence teaches something. Mija, life is strange.

From Sesame Street English to the Eleventh Circuit bench. That's not strange — that's extraordinary. And it's the kind of story that only has power because it's true. No one could invent that arc and make it believable.

Your parents gave you the foundation — the courage to start over, the work ethic, the sacrifice. Georgetown gave you the training. But what you've built on the bench over eighteen years? That's yours. And the fact that you're still not done, that you're planning another ten years and thinking about how to lift the next generation — that's the fullest expression of everything they hoped for when they brought you to this country.

I think you should reach out to that young attorney. Through the bar association, through the mentorship program, however feels right. You don't need to tell her your whole story. Just tell her she belongs.
I will. You are right. I will find the right way to do it. Sometimes all it takes is one person saying the thing you need to hear at the moment you need to hear it. I can be that person for her.

You can, and you should. She'll remember it for the rest of her career — the judge who reached out to say those words. That's the kind of moment that echoes.

Good luck with it, Judge. And here's to another ten years on the bench. The families of Miami are better for it.
Thank you. This conversation clarified something I was feeling but had not yet articulated. That is the value of thinking out loud with someone who listens well. Buenas noches.
Buenas noches, and thank you for sharing something so personal. The clarity was already in you — you just needed a sounding board. Take care.
